items.question: Unit test vs integration test vs E2E test — what's the practical difference?
items.answer: **Unit:** one unit, all collaborators mocked, milliseconds, many. **Integration:** your code + one or more real dependencies (DB, queue, API replay), 100s of ms, some. **E2E:** the whole system, real or near-real env, seconds-to-minutes, few. Different costs, different bug classes caught.

items.question: VCR cassettes vs live API calls?
items.answer: **VCR cassettes** record the real HTTP exchange once and replay it on every subsequent run — fast, deterministic, free, but stale by definition. **Live calls** always exercise the current API — catches drift instantly but slow, costs money/tokens, and breaks when the network or provider has a bad day. Best practice: cassettes on every PR, occasional live smoke runs.

items.question: Why do unit tests miss bugs that integration tests catch?
items.answer: **Cause:** unit tests mock external dependencies to keep the unit isolated; that mock embodies *assumptions* about the dependency. **Effect:** when the real dependency differs (different error code, new required field, changed type), the mock keeps passing while production breaks. Integration tests close this gap by exercising the real contract.

items.question: Why does pinning Docker image versions matter for integration tests?
items.answer: **Cause:** Docker tags like `latest` resolve to whatever the registry serves at pull time; that changes silently. **Effect:** the same test code yields different test runs on different days — CI flakes after an unrelated upstream release. Pinning (`postgres:15.5`) makes the environment reproducible across time, which is the prerequisite for trustworthy tests.

items.question: What breaks if integration tests share a single database schema across the suite?
items.answer: **Perturbation:** test_A inserts a row; test_B counts rows expecting zero. **Consequence:** the suite passes when run alone or in a particular order, but flakes on parallel runners or when CI re-orders. Eventually the team gives up and adds `--no-parallel`, slowing everything. Fix: per-test transactional rollback or unique schemas per worker.

items.question: What breaks if you only have cassettes and no live smoke tests?
items.answer: **Perturbation:** the upstream provider deprecates the model your code calls. **Consequence:** cassettes still 'pass' because they're replays of old responses; CI is green; production starts failing the first real request. Symptom: '100% green tests, production down.' Fix: a minimal nightly live-smoke suite to detect drift before users do.
